items.headline: SpaceX flies DARPA-backed mission to refuel aging geostationary satellites
items.title: SpaceX launches Northrop mission to extend the life of aging satellites
items.scifi_hook: A private rocket docks with three old satellites to give them new propulsion pods in orbit.
items.eli5: SpaceX sent a spacecraft that attaches thrusters to three old satellites so they can stay in space longer.
items.why: Extending satellite life reduces launch costs and space debris.
items.rationale: Northrop may see contract revenue growth
items.text: DARPA-backed vehicle will install propulsion pods on three commercial satellites in geostationary orbit The post SpaceX launches Northrop mission to extend the life of aging satellites appeared first on SpaceNews .

items.headline: NISAR radar captures ‘Hummingbird’ image of Antarctic ice flow
items.title: NISAR’s L-Band Radar Reveals ‘Hummingbird’ in Antarctica
items.scifi_hook: A joint US‑India radar satellite snaps a vivid picture of a mountain poking through Antarctic ice.
items.eli5: The NISAR satellite used radar waves to map a nunatak that looks like a hummingbird in the ice.
items.why: Helps scientists understand ice stress and potential sea‑level rise.
items.text: 2 Min Read NISAR’s L-Band Radar Reveals ‘Hummingbird’ in Antarctica PIA26617 Credits: NASA/JPL-Caltech Photojournal Navigation Science Photojournal NISAR’s L-Band Radar Reveals… Photojournal Home Photojournal Search Latest Content Galleries Feedback RSS About Downloads NISAR’s L-Band Radar Reveals ‘Hummingbird’ in Antarctica PNG (43.77 MB) PIA26617 Figure A JPEG (29.10 MB) Description Data from the Earth-orbiting U.S.-India NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite’s L-band radar was used to produce an image of Nunatak Zaterjavshijsja — a mountaintop in East Antarctica — poking out amid a stream of ice flowing northeast to the ocean. The obstruction causes stresses in the ice, heavily fracturing the surrounding surfaces with deep cracks, called crevasses, which show as sharp green lines in the image. Produced in August 2025, the image has been nicknamed “the hummingbird” by NISAR scientists. The colors show differences in the way polarized microwave signals, which vibrate in different directions, interact with and reflect from the ice. Over Antarctica, NISAR transmits radar waves toward Earth with a horizontal polarization. The orientation of the signals that return, either horizontal, vertical, or both, provide clues about the object or surface that reflected them. Signals that come back with a horizontal polarization likely bounced off a more regular surface, such as smooth ice. Those signals appear magenta in the image. items.headline: New Zealand startup Zenno Astronautics moves to the United States
items.title: Zenno Astronautics to relocate to the United States
items.scifi_hook: A Kiwi firm with superconducting magnets for spacecraft control relocates to America.
items.eli5: Zenno Astronautics, which uses powerful magnets to steer rockets, is shifting its base to the US.
items.why: U.S. relocation may give the company better funding and market access.
items.text: SAN FRANCISCO – Zenno Astronautics, a New Zealand startup focused on applying superconducting magnets for spacecraft attitude control and acceleration, plans to redomicile to the United States. “It is obvious […] The post Zenno Astronautics to relocate to the United States appeared first on SpaceNews .

items.headline: Two‑thirds of global mines face serious water‑stress risk, ICMM finds
items.title: Majority of mining and metals facilities face significant water risk, ICMM study shows
items.scifi_hook: A new study shows most mining sites sit on water‑scarce ground, threatening future production.
items.eli5: About 66% of mines are in places where water is already limited or over‑used.
items.why: Water scarcity could constrain supply of critical minerals for clean‑energy technologies.
items.rationale: Water risk may pressure operating costs
items.text: Nearly two-thirds of mining and metals facilities worldwide are located in areas facing significant physical water risk, according to new analysis by the ICMM. Its Global Mining and Metals Water Dataset and report also reveals that competition for water between mining and metals operations and other industrial and domestic users is the greatest physical water risk facing the industry. It highlights the importance of coordinated integrated land and water use planning at regional scales, as demand grows for the materials needed for clean energy systems. Minerals and metals are essential to the energy transition and the low-carbon economy of the future, but this analysis shows that the conditions needed to produce them are constrained by physical water risks, the ICMM says. Key findings include: 65.7% of mining and metals facilities globally are exposed to high levels of at least one physical water risk indicator; 38.2% of facilities are located in catchments with high or extremely high baseline water stress, or in arid areas where water is functionally unavailable; 27.4% of facilities globally are exposed to high or extremely high baseline water depletion; 27% of facilities globally are exposed to high drought risk, making drought the second most prevalent physical water risk analysed; 14% of facilities globally are exposed to high or extremely high flood risk, although exposure is higher for alumina refining, aluminium smelting, molybdenum production and steel production; 16.2

items.headline: Covalent Lithium to double Mt Holland spodumene output by 2030
items.title: Covalent Lithium to double spodumene output from Mt Holland
items.scifi_hook: A joint venture will double lithium‑rich ore production, adding a new ore‑sorting plant.
items.eli5: The project will increase lithium concentrate from 380,000 to 760,000 tonnes per year and add a plant to process more ore.
items.why: Higher output supports growing demand for lithium batteries.
items.text: Wesfarmers Limited, together with Sociedad Quimica y Minera de Chile S.A (SQM), has announced the joint approval of the final investment decision to expand the Mt Holland lithium project, comprising the mine, concentrator and a new integrated ore sorting facility. The expansion will double spodumene concentrate production, lower unit operating costs and accelerate cash flows, the companies say. Covalent Lithium, the joint venture company owned by Wesfarmers and SQM, has completed a definitive feasibility study for the expansion project, which will increase nameplate spodumene concentrate production from approximately 380,000 t/y to 760,000 t/y on a 100% basis. An ore sorting facility will also be constructed to recover stockpiled material currently unsuitable for processing, adding approximately 3 Mt of spodumene concentrate production over the life of the operation. Construction of a second concentrator is expected to commence in the second half of calendar year 2027, with the first spodumene concentrate production volumes from the expansion expected in the first half of calendar year 2030. Wesfarmers’ share of capital expenditure for the project is estimated to be between $450 million and $500 million in nominal terms and excluding capitalised interest, and will be funded using existing cash and debt facilities. The necessary government and regulatory approvals have either been secured or are underway. The expanded production volumes are expected to be sold as spodumene con

items.headline: AI could add $228 billion annually to US construction sector by 2030
items.title: AI could unlock $228 billion in annual value for US AEC sector by 2030: McKinsey
items.scifi_hook: Artificial intelligence promises a trillion‑dollar boost to building design and engineering.
items.eli5: Using AI tools could create $228 billion of extra value each year for U.S. architects, engineers and builders.
items.why: Automation can cut costs and speed up project delivery.
items.text: Artificial intelligence (AI) and other automation technologies could unlock around $228 billion in annual value for the US architecture, engineering and construction (AEC) sector by 2030, while AI's impact on the European construction sector alone could reach approximately $126 billion, according to a McKinsey report.

items.scifi_hook: Machine‑learning models are sharpening predictions of storms and climate events worldwide.
items.eli5: Artificial intelligence helps meteorologists predict weather more accurately by processing huge data sets.
items.why: Better forecasts improve safety and economic planning across sectors.
